title: Best Drill Bits for Stainless Steel in 2026: Genuine Cobalt That Won't Quit
excerpt: Find the best drill bits for stainless steel that cut clean holes without burning up—for DIYers and metalworkers drilling hard metal with confidence.
author: Editorial Team
metaDescription: Find the best drill bits for stainless steel that cut clean holes without burning up—for DIYers and metalworkers drilling hard metal with confidence.
intro:
Many drill bits sold as 'stainless steel capable' turn out to be titanium-coated high-speed steel that dulls after a single hole. The culprit isn't poor workmanship—it's the intense heat and hardness of stainless, which demands a different bit material altogether.

This guide separates the marketing claims from the metal. You'll know exactly which bits are built for repeated stainless work and which are best saved for wood and plastic. The simple adjustments—low speed, steady pressure, and cutting oil—can double the life of any bit, but matching the bit to the job is what keeps a project moving forward.

products.title: Bosch CO14 14 pc. Cobalt Metal Drill Bit Set
products.label: Bosch CO14 Cobalt Set
products.oneLiner: Bosch CO14's genuine M42-grade cobalt and 3-flat shank grip stainless steel without walking.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Always use cutting oil and a drill press when possible; cobalt's heat resistance works best at low RPMs to avoid brittle failure.
pros: Bites into stainless steel, cast aluminum, and mild steel without walking or chattering. — 236 mentions for drill performance, 86% positive
Build quality and sizing consistency outclass many similarly-priced cobalt sets. — 356 mentions, 94% positive
Edge retention stays sharp after repeated holes, reducing replacement frequency. — 137 mentions for sharpness, 77% positive; 169 for durability, 73% positive
cons: Cobalt's extreme hardness makes bits brittle; flexing, dropping, or off-angle hand drilling can snap smaller diameters. — 169 durability mentions, 27% negative — occasional snap when misused
products.verdict: If stainless steel is a regular drilling target and you can give it a drill press or rock-steady hand with cutting oil, the CO14 delivers professional-grade results that TiN sets cannot match.
products.review:
M42-grade cobalt steel and a split-point tip allow these bits to start on contact in hardened stainless, cast iron, and alloy steel without a center punch. The 3-flat shank locks securely into the chuck, preventing slip on larger sizes. Cutting edges hold up through repeated holes in material that dull standard TiN bits within seconds. Slow speeds and cutting oil produce clean, burr-free results.

This set targets metalworkers and fabricators who drill stainless steel regularly and are comfortable with the discipline of low RPMs, cutting oil, and a drill press or very stable hand. It's not for wood-only shops or those unwilling to adapt technique — lateral force or a drop can snap smaller bits. For that tradeoff, you get a durable, imperial-sizes kit in a well-organized case.

💡 💡 Tip: Always use cutting oil and a drill press when possible; cobalt's heat resistance works best at low RPMs to avoid brittle failure.

products.title: DEWALT Cobalt Alloy Steel Drill Bit Set with Pilot Point, 14-Piece (DWA1240)
products.label: DEWALT DWA1240 Cobalt Set
products.oneLiner: Pilot-point cobalt alloy set that starts cleanly on steel without a center punch.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Use cutting oil and moderate RPMs on stainless to extend edge life.
pros: Cobalt alloy steel cuts thick stainless and alloy steel with minimal effort. — 71 cutting performance mentions, 89% positive
Pilot point tips start holes cleanly without a center punch, reducing walk on metal surfaces. — Many reviews reference no-walk starts on metal
cons: Edge retention can be shorter under sustained high-RPM stainless drilling, particularly compared to the Bosch CO14 set. — 47 sharpness mentions, 47% negative — quick dulling reported by a subset of users
products.verdict: A solid cobalt set for DEWALT users upgrading from TiN bits; the pilot point saves setup time, though edge life may not match the Bosch CO14 in continuous production drilling.
products.review:
Unlike DEWALT's TiN-coated generalist bits, the DWA1240 uses genuine cobalt alloy steel to handle thick stainless and alloy steels that wear down lesser bits. The 14-piece set spans 1/16 to 1/2 inch, making it a practical upgrade for DEWALT loyalists who drill stainless regularly and want the speed of a pilot point that starts cleanly without a center punch. Edge life lags behind the top-performing Bosch CO14 under sustained high-speed use — a tradeoff for the convenience and brand familiarity.

💡 💡 Tip: Use cutting oil and moderate RPMs on stainless to extend edge life.

products.title: Metric M42 8% Cobalt Twist Drill Bits Set for Stainless Steel and Hard Metal (1mm-10mm/19pcs)
products.label: STROTON M42 Metric Set
products.oneLiner: M42 8% cobalt set in 0.5mm increments for hardened stainless and alloy steel.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Reserve the sub-2 mm bits for use in a drill press or with a rigid guide — hand-held wobble can snap them.
pros: M42 cobalt at 68-70 HRC stays sharp through hardened stainless and tough alloys. — 75 cutting performance mentions, 76% positive
Metric 0.5 mm increments cover precise sizing for European and Asian machinery. — 35 material compatibility mentions, 71% positive
135° split point design starts fast and minimizes walking in metal.
cons: Sub-2 mm bits are extremely fragile and may snap under off-center hand drilling; treat them as consumables. — 129 durability mentions, 68% negative
products.verdict: Metric precision and M42 hardness make this the set for European car and machinery work, provided you treat the smallest bits with the care they demand.
products.review:
The 19-piece STROTON set uses M42 8% cobalt to achieve a 68-70 HRC hardness that slices through hardened stainless, tool steel, and titanium alloys. The 135° split point bites immediately and resists walking, while metric sizing from 1 mm to 10 mm in 0.5 mm steps covers the precision needs of European and Asian machinery.

Mechanics and machinists working on metric fasteners and diesel components will appreciate the specific increments and heat tolerance. The sub-2 mm bits, however, are delicate — treat them as consumables and avoid off-center hand drilling to prevent snapping. This set suits users who primarily drill larger metric holes and can handle small bits with care.

💡 💡 Tip: Reserve the sub-2 mm bits for use in a drill press or with a rigid guide — hand-held wobble can snap them.

products.review: This 12-pack of identical 1/4-inch M35 cobalt bits is purpose-built for repetitive stainless work. Unlike the top pick that covers a range of sizes with M42 cobalt, Drill Hulk focuses on a single diameter to slash the per-hole cost for trailer builders, automotive shops, and anyone drilling numerous 1/4-inch bolt or rivet holes. The tradeoff: you'll need a separate index for other sizes. But if your job is drilling through 304 stainless plate all day, this bulk pack keeps you in fresh bits at an unbeatable price.

products.title: DEWALT Drill Bit Set, 21 Pc. Titanium Nitride Coated Pilot Point DEWALT Bit Set with Case for Wood, Metal and Plastic (DW1361)
products.label: DEWALT DW1361 TiN Set
products.oneLiner: 21-piece TiN-coated pilot-point set covering wood, plastic, and occasional stainless steel.
products.usageNote: 💡 Tip: Use cutting oil and lower RPM when drilling stainless steel to extend bit life and reduce breakage.
pros: Pilot point reduces wandering, starts cleanly without center punch. — 379 mentions for functionality, 95% positive
Solid value for a name-brand 21-piece kit. — 434 mentions, 94% positive
Sharp out of the box through wood, plastic, and mild steel. — 190 mentions, 83% positive
cons: Smaller bits can snap under heavy load, particularly on stainless steel without proper speed and lubrication. — 538 mentions, 37% negative — occasional breakage reported, especially when used without proper speed/lubrication
products.verdict: A smart pick if you need one set for occasional stainless drilling among wood and plastic projects, but dedicated cobalt bits are the better investment for frequent metalwork.
products.review:
The pilot point tip bites into material without a center punch, minimizing wandering on curved or slick surfaces. Across 21 sizes from 1/16 to 1/2 inch, the hex shanks grip well in chucks, and the TiN coating reduces friction in wood, plastic, and mild steel. When used on stainless steel, the coating wears faster and the bits dull more quickly than genuine cobalt — acceptable for occasional holes, but not for production work.

This set suits homeowners who drill mixed materials and need a single comprehensive kit. Anyone drilling stainless steel regularly should step up to a cobalt set like DEWALT’s M35/M42 option, because the TiN coating cannot be resharpened once worn.

💡 💡 Tip: Use cutting oil and lower RPM when drilling stainless steel to extend bit life and reduce breakage.

Fifteen genuine M35 cobalt 1/4-inch bits offer the lowest per-bit cost for shops that drill repeated holes in stainless steel sheet and tube. The 135-degree split point bites quickly without a center punch, and the 5% cobalt alloy resists heat better than standard high-speed steel. Compared to the top pick's M42 build, these bits trade a bit of edge life for a much larger quantity at a budget-friendly price.

This set is built for fabricators who run through 1/4-inch bits on production stainless assemblies. Longevity under continuous heavy use can vary — occasional snapping has been noted — but the pack assumes some breakage and keeps spares close. Shops that value consumable cost per hole over maximum individual bit life will find the math works.

buyingGuide.heading: Material: Cobalt vs. Titanium Nitride
buyingGuide.body:
Genuine cobalt steel (M35 or M42) is the non-negotiable standard for stainless. The cobalt alloy retains its hardness even as friction heats the tip, while a titanium nitride coating on HSS softens and wears away under the same heat. Once the coating goes, the underlying high-speed steel dulls rapidly.

Cobalt bits can be resharpened multiple times, extending their useful life. TiN-coated bits cannot be re-sharpened without stripping the coating. If you're drilling more than a few holes, the upfront cost of cobalt translates to a lower cost-per-hole.

buyingGuide.heading: Point Style: Split-Point vs. Pilot Point
buyingGuide.body:
Split-point bits have a chisel edge that reduces walking and self-center on hard surfaces without a center punch. This geometry also requires less feed pressure, which is a major advantage when hand-drilling stainless. Pilot points start cleanly in softer metals but may walk on polished stainless if pressure is uneven.

The split-point's sharper cutting angle generates less heat at the point of contact, helping preserve the bit's edge. If you often skip a center punch, a split-point design will save you from ruined starts on stainless sheet.

buyingGuide.heading: Shank Type: 3-Flat, Hex, or Straight
buyingGuide.body:
A 3-flat shank adds three flattened sides that prevent the bit from spinning in the chuck, especially on larger diameters under high torque. Straight shanks are adequate for bits under ¼ inch but may slip during heavy stainless drilling. Hex shanks are mainly for impact drivers and quick-change chucks, but the bits are typically shorter and not suited for deep holes.

If you use a keyless chuck on a hand drill, a 3-flat shank can be the difference between a clean hole and a smoking, stuck bit. For drill press work, a straight shank is perfectly fine.

buyingGuide.heading: Size Range and Quantity
buyingGuide.body:
Large sets with every fractional size look appealing, but in practice you'll wear through the most-used diameters—1/8", 3/16", and 1/4"—long before the larger sizes see action. Buying bulk packs of those key sizes and a smaller set for the rest often provides more value.

Small-diameter bits under 1/8" are inherently fragile in any material. Treat them as consumables, keep spares, and never force them. A 10-pack of the same size can cost less than replacing a full set after a single broken bit.

faq.question: Can titanium drill bits drill stainless steel?
faq.answer: Only for very thin sheet metal and occasional holes. The titanium nitride coating reduces friction but won't prevent the underlying high-speed steel from softening under the heat generated by stainless. For any repeated or substantial work, genuine cobalt is required. Step bits with TiN coating are particularly prone to edge rounding on stainless.

faq.question: Why do my cobalt drill bits keep breaking when drilling stainless steel?
faq.answer: Cobalt steel is hard but also brittle. Most breaks come from side pressure when using a hand drill, running at high RPM without cutting oil, or failing to back out to clear chips. Use a drill press when possible, keep speed under 500 RPM, and apply steady, straight pressure. A 3-flat shank in a keyless chuck helps prevent the bit from seizing and snapping.

faq.question: What is the difference between M35 and M42 cobalt drill bits?
faq.answer: M35 contains 5% cobalt and handles most stainless, alloy steel, and cast iron. M42 has 8% cobalt and retains its hardness better under the extreme heat of production drilling in hardened stainless or tool steel. For home shops and occasional use, M35 is sufficient; if you're drilling thick hardened fasteners all day, M42 extends bit life noticeably.

faq.question: Are step drill bits good for stainless steel?
faq.answer: Only if the step bit is made from cobalt and used at very low speed with lubrication. Most step bits are TiN-coated HSS and will dull or burn up quickly on stainless. Even cobalt steps have a limited reach and cannot drill deep holes. For repeated stainless work, twist bits are the better, longer-lasting choice.
